text: The mitochondrion synthesizes ATP through oxidative phosphorylation along its inner membrane.
Within the inner membrane, the electron transport chain passes electrons from NADH and FADH2 toward molecular oxygen.
As electrons traverse the electron transport chain, protons accumulate in the intermembrane space and build an electrochemical gradient.
ATP synthase exploits this proton gradient to phosphorylate ADP into ATP.
The citric acid cycle in the mitochondrial matrix supplies the NADH and FADH2 that feed the electron transport chain.
Pyruvate from glycolysis enters the mitochondrion and is oxidized to acetyl-CoA before the citric acid cycle.
Each mitochondrion carries its own circular DNA, which encodes several subunits of the electron transport chain.
When the proton gradient collapses, ATP synthase slows and the mitochondrion produces far less ATP.
Reactive oxygen species leak from the electron transport chain and can damage mitochondrial DNA and the inner membrane.
Cells rich in mitochondria, such as cardiac muscle, demand abundant ATP from oxidative phosphorylation.
A main-sequence star fuses hydrogen into helium in its core through the proton-proton chain.
Radiation pressure from hydrogen fusion balances the star's gravity and keeps the core stable.
When core hydrogen is exhausted, fusion migrates to a shell and the star swells into a red giant.
In a massive star, successive fusion stages forge carbon, oxygen, and finally iron in the core.
Iron releases no energy through fusion, so the core collapses and the star detonates as a supernova.
The supernova scatters heavy elements and leaves behind a neutron star or a black hole.
A neutron star compresses more than a solar mass into a sphere kilometers across, held up by neutron degeneracy pressure.
If the remnant exceeds roughly three solar masses, gravity overwhelms degeneracy pressure and a black hole forms.
The intense gravity of a black hole warps spacetime and traps light inside the event horizon.
A spinning neutron star sweeps beams of radiation across the sky and is observed as a pulsar.
A carbon atom forms four covalent bonds, giving organic molecules their structural diversity.
Hydrocarbons contain only carbon and hydrogen, joined by single, double, or triple covalent bonds.
A double bond between carbon atoms makes an alkene more reactive than the corresponding alkane.
The hydroxyl group defines an alcohol, while the carbonyl group appears in aldehydes and ketones.
A carboxylic acid combines a carbonyl group and a hydroxyl group on the same carbon atom.
In esterification, a carboxylic acid reacts with an alcohol to form an ester and water.
A nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon of a carbonyl group during an addition reaction.
Benzene's delocalized electrons form an aromatic ring that resists the addition reactions typical of an alkene.
A functional group such as the hydroxyl group or the carbonyl group dictates how an organic molecule reacts.
Polymers form when many monomers join through repeated covalent bonds along a carbon backbone.
When a pathogen breaches the skin, the innate immune system mounts the first response.
A macrophage engulfs the pathogen and displays its antigens on the cell surface.
A helper T cell recognizes the antigen presented by the macrophage and becomes activated.
The activated helper T cell secretes cytokines that stimulate B cells and killer T cells.
A B cell that binds the antigen differentiates into a plasma cell and secretes antibodies.
Antibodies bind the pathogen, neutralize it, and mark it for destruction by a phagocyte.
A killer T cell destroys infected host cells that display the pathogen's antigens.
After the infection clears, memory B cells and memory T cells persist for years.
On re-exposure, the memory cells recognize the antigen and produce antibodies far faster.
A vaccine primes this memory response by presenting harmless antigens to the immune system.
Earth's lithosphere is fractured into rigid plates that drift over the ductile asthenosphere.
Convection currents in the mantle drive the slow motion of the tectonic plates.
At a divergent boundary, two plates separate and magma rises to form new oceanic crust.
At a convergent boundary, a dense oceanic plate subducts beneath a lighter continental plate.
Subduction drags the oceanic plate into the mantle, where it melts and feeds a volcanic arc.
The friction of subduction stores stress along a fault until it releases as an earthquake.
At a transform boundary, two plates grind past each other along a fault.
Magma that reaches the surface erupts from a volcano and cools into basalt.
Seafloor spreading at a divergent boundary pushes the plates apart and widens the ocean basin.
The slow drift of the plates rearranges the continents over millions of years.
A neural network maps inputs to outputs through layers of weighted connections.
Each neuron computes a weighted sum of its inputs and applies a nonlinear activation function.
Training adjusts the weights to minimize a loss function over the training data.
Gradient descent updates each weight in the direction that reduces the loss.
Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight.
The learning rate controls how large a step gradient descent takes at each update.
A network with too many weights can overfit the training data and generalize poorly.
Regularization penalizes large weights and helps the neural network generalize to new data.
An activation function such as ReLU lets the neural network model nonlinear relationships.
Stacking many layers gives a deep neural network the capacity to learn rich features.
